title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Antimicrobial Resistance: The Efficacy Ceiling and the System Welfare Cost of Antibiotic Depletion
epigraph: The healing and the depletion are the same molecular event.
executiveSummary:
The global antibiotic ecosystem destroys approximately $2.10 of system welfare for every dollar of private payoff at the margin. The system-adjusted payoff is -$380 billion at full welfare pricing. This paper establishes these measurements by applying the System Asset Pricing Model—the continuous measurement extension of the Private Pareto Theorem—to the global antibiotic ecosystem. It computes the Private-Systemic Frontier, system betas, system-adjusted payoffs, and crossover times across six channels of Private-Systemic Tension. All six pillars of the system welfare function are monetized in dollars per year. The classification is Slow Hollow Win with catastrophic discontinuity risk: the effectiveness commons depletes gradually across the aggregate, but individual drug-pathogen combinations collapse discontinuously when resistance crosses clinical thresholds. Channel-level system betas range from 0.8 for the environmental reservoir to 5.2 for inappropriate prescribing. The weighted aggregate system beta is 1.60; the Private-Systemic Frontier-derived marginal aggregate system beta is 2.1, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [1.6, 2.6] for uncorrelated draws and [1.4, 2.9] for correlated draws. Each dollar of private payoff, estimated at $550 billion per year, depletes approximately $1.60 of system welfare on average and $2.10 at the margin. At full social-cost pricing, the system-adjusted payoff is -$380 billion per year. The equilibrium classifies as a Slow Hollow Win with catastrophic discontinuity risk, a classification invariant across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws. Zero of these draws produced a welfare-positive outcome, confirming the robustness of the system-destructive verdict. This paper also proves the Efficacy Ceiling, the twenty-first impossibility theorem in the economics canon. This theorem establishes that no prescribing protocol, stewardship program, or new drug pipeline operating within a therapeutic system satisfying three defining axioms of antibiotic therapy—Therapeutic Lethality (R1), Evolutionary Selection (R2), and Resistance Mobility (R3)—can guarantee that population-level antibiotic effectiveness remains above any fixed threshold as cumulative use increases. The result follows from the conjunction of these axioms: the healing and the depletion are the same molecular event. Fourteen escape-closure propositions (EC-2 through EC-15) prove that no within-system mechanism can evade the Efficacy Ceiling. The Welfare Gap (EC-7) derives a quantitative depletion bound, directly connecting the impossibility theorem to the System Asset Pricing Model measurement. This establishes that a system beta greater than zero is a mathematical consequence of the axioms, not merely an empirical finding. The economic burden of antimicrobial resistance is substantial. Bacterial antimicrobial resistance caused approximately 1.27 million attributable deaths in 2019, with 4.95 million deaths associated with resistant infections. Projections for 2050 indicate approximately 1.9 million attributable deaths. The O'Neill Review in 2016 projected up to 10 million antimicrobial resistance deaths per year by 2050 with a cumulative $100 trillion gross domestic product loss. The World Bank in 2017 warned that antimicrobial resistance could add $1 trillion annually to healthcare costs by 2050 and cost $1 to $3.4 trillion of gross domestic product per year by 2030. Global hospital costs attributable to antibiotic resistance were estimated at $693 billion in 2019, with approximately $194 billion in productivity losses. On the private side, the global human antibiotics market generates approximately $55 billion in annual revenue, with the veterinary antibiotics market contributing roughly $5 billion more. However, these revenue numbers understate the relevant private payoff, as most of the annual gain accrues as immediate use-value: infections cured, admissions shortened, livestock losses avoided, and procedures safely undertaken because prophylaxis still works. Using a broader ledger that includes direct market revenues and the immediate private value of medically enabled activity, the present annual aggregate private payoff is estimated at approximately $550 billion. The System Asset Pricing Model framework constructs a monetized system welfare function covering six channels: inappropriate clinical prescribing, appropriate-but-depleting clinical prescribing, livestock prophylaxis and metaphylaxis, pipeline depletion, hospital resistance amplification, and the environmental reservoir. The annual welfare shortfall, representing the avoidable damage above the cooperative baseline, is approximately $930 billion. The difference of $2.66 trillion between the total welfare cost ($3.59 trillion) and the welfare shortfall ($930 billion) represents the irreducible damage from resistance generated by medically necessary use, which persists even under optimal stewardship. This is precisely the empirical content of the Efficacy Ceiling: even the cooperative baseline cannot eliminate the bulk of the welfare cost because Axiom R1 guarantees that every therapeutic use creates selection pressure. The paper also highlights the Danish model as a documented game transformation. Denmark eliminated growth-promotion antibiotics by 2000 and achieved a greater than 50 percent reduction in total livestock antibiotic use with minimal productivity loss. This demonstrates that local commons governance can shift behavior and reduce system welfare costs. However, the Partial Coalition Insufficiency proposition (EC-4) proves that local stewardship cannot close a globally connected commons when Axiom R3 ensures that resistance selected outside the boundary reaches inside it through travel, trade, food supply chains, and environmental contamination. The gap between local governance and global commons management is the gap the Efficacy Ceiling formalizes. This research provides the first unified welfare accounting that prices all channels in a common numéraire and maps them onto a single tradeoff frontier. It extends the System Asset Pricing Model to a biological commons where the system variable (effectiveness) is non-renewable, the depletion mechanism is constitutive of the domain, and the institutional response is fragmented. The Efficacy Ceiling contributes a new structural impossibility result to the economics literature. The measurements are now in: the commons is being spent faster than it needs to be, and the institutional structures that would slow the spending do not yet exist at the scale the biology demands.

keyFindings: The marginal system beta for the global antibiotic ecosystem is 2.1. This means that for every dollar of private payoff generated by antibiotic use, approximately $2.10 of system welfare is depleted from the effectiveness commons at the margin. This ratio is derived from the curvature of the Private-Systemic Frontier, which maps the tradeoff between private gains and system welfare. The weighted average system beta across all six channels is 1.60. This finding confirms that the antibiotic ecosystem operates under severe Private-Systemic Tension, where private gains systematically lead to greater system losses. The system beta is not merely an empirical observation but is shown to be a mathematical consequence of the fundamental biological axioms governing antibiotic action, as formalized by the Welfare Gap proposition (EC-7). The magnitude of this beta indicates a highly unsustainable extraction rate from a critical common-pool resource.

The system-adjusted payoff for the global antibiotic ecosystem is -$380 billion per year at full welfare pricing. This figure is calculated by subtracting the annual welfare shortfall from the aggregate private payoff. The aggregate private payoff is estimated at $550 billion per year, encompassing human and veterinary market revenues, the value of infections cured, and the value of surgeries and other procedures enabled by antibiotic prophylaxis. The annual welfare shortfall, representing the avoidable damage above the cooperative baseline, is approximately $930 billion. The deeply negative system-adjusted payoff indicates that, when the full costs of depleting antibiotic effectiveness are accounted for, the current operation of the antibiotic ecosystem destroys significantly more value than it creates. This negative payoff is robust across 100,000 Monte Carlo simulations, with zero draws yielding a welfare-positive outcome.

This paper proves the Efficacy Ceiling, the twenty-first impossibility theorem in the economics canon. This theorem establishes that no prescribing protocol, stewardship program, or new drug pipeline operating within a therapeutic system satisfying three defining axioms of antibiotic therapy—Therapeutic Lethality (R1), Evolutionary Selection (R2), and Resistance Mobility (R3)—can guarantee that population-level antibiotic effectiveness remains above any fixed threshold as cumulative use increases. The core insight is that the healing effect of an antibiotic and its depletion effect (selection for resistance) are the same molecular event. This structural impossibility is stronger than the familiar claim that resistance is difficult to manage; it states that, under ordinary biological laws, threshold preservation cannot be guaranteed from within the use-regime itself. The theorem's formal proof is presented in Section 6 and Appendix A, demonstrating its deductive certainty.

The global antibiotic ecosystem is classified as a Slow Hollow Win with catastrophic discontinuity risk. This classification means that while the aggregate effectiveness commons depletes gradually over time, individual drug-pathogen combinations can and do collapse discontinuously when resistance crosses critical clinical thresholds. This is analogous to the Electric Reliability Council of Texas boundary case, where average grid reliability masked the step-function failure of individual generators during extreme weather events. The classification is invariant across 100,000 Monte Carlo draws, with 100 percent of simulations confirming the Slow Hollow Win outcome. This robustness underscores that the system's fundamental dynamics lead to welfare destruction, regardless of reasonable variations in underlying parameters. The catastrophic discontinuity risk highlights that aggregate metrics can obscure acute, life-threatening failures at the patient level.

The Danish model provides empirical evidence of a documented game transformation from a Hollow Win toward a more sustainable equilibrium in the livestock channel. Denmark banned avoparcin, a vancomycin analog used as a growth promoter, in 1995, and all remaining growth promoters by 2000. This led to a greater than 50 percent reduction in total livestock antibiotic use, with agricultural productivity maintained or even increased within two to three years. Surveillance data from DANMAP documented measurable declines in vancomycin-resistant enterococci in livestock after avoparcin withdrawal. This intervention targeted a channel with a high system beta (livestock prophylaxis, 2.5), demonstrating that strategic policy can reduce welfare destruction by eliminating low-value extraction. The Danish experience serves as a proof-of-concept that institutional redesign can shift the Private-Systemic Frontier, even if Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility) means that global coordination is ultimately required to fully escape the Efficacy Ceiling.

The Universal Escape Impossibility (Proposition EC-8) systematically proves that no within-system mechanism can evade the Efficacy Ceiling. This apparatus includes fourteen propositions (EC-2 through EC-15) that close every conceivable escape route. Specifically, optimal stewardship protocols (EC-2) cannot guarantee effectiveness preservation because even minimum therapeutic doses create selection pressure. Drug cycling, mixing, or rotation strategies (EC-3) fail because each class depletes independently, and cross-resistance couples classes through Resistance Mobility (R3). Partial coalitions of stewardship jurisdictions (EC-4) are insufficient because R3 ensures resistance from non-participating jurisdictions will propagate globally. Prescribing mechanisms (EC-5) cannot work without independent population-level surveillance, as patient-level data alone are insufficient to track global resistance. Repeated cooperation (EC-6) in game theory does not escape the Ceiling, as cumulative selection pressure still diverges. Finally, the new drug pipeline (EC-3 Corollary) cannot restore effectiveness for depleted classes, as new drugs merely create new effectiveness for new classes, rather than refilling the ocean of existing effectiveness. All these failures stem from the constitutive identity of therapy and depletion established by Axiom R1.

The Welfare Gap (Proposition EC-7) provides a quantitative depletion bound, formally connecting the Efficacy Ceiling impossibility theorem to the System Asset Pricing Model measurement. It establishes that the system beta for appropriate prescribing (βW) is necessarily greater than zero, not merely an empirical finding, but a mathematical consequence of Axioms R1, R2, and R3. Specifically, W(t) ≤ W(0) − βW · Π(t), where W(t) is system welfare at time t, W(0) is initial welfare, βW is system beta, and Π(t) is cumulative private payoff. This means that every therapeutically effective prescription necessarily depletes the commons. The empirical estimate of βW = 0.57 for appropriate prescribing substantially exceeds this theoretical lower bound because the per-prescription resistance cost compounds through irreversibility (R2) and global propagation (R3). This proposition unifies the theoretical impossibility with the empirical measurement, demonstrating that the negative system-adjusted payoff is guaranteed by the underlying biological axioms.

Channel-level system betas exhibit significant variation, ranging from 0.8 for the environmental reservoir to 5.2 for inappropriate clinical prescribing. Inappropriate clinical prescribing (Channel 1) has the highest system beta of 5.2, indicating that each dollar of perceived private benefit from unnecessary prescriptions destroys $5.20 of system welfare. Livestock prophylaxis (Channel 3) has a system beta of 2.5, meaning each dollar of private payoff from agricultural antibiotic use generates $2.50 of system welfare cost, largely due to the use of medically critical antibiotics for non-therapeutic purposes. Hospital resistance amplification (Channel 5) has a system beta of 2.3, reflecting hospitals' role as incubators of resistance. In contrast, appropriate clinical prescribing (Channel 2) has a system beta of 0.57, below unit Private-Systemic Tension, but its enormous aggregate volume makes it the largest contributor to absolute welfare cost. Pipeline depletion (Channel 4) has a system beta of 0.55, also below unit Private-Systemic Tension, but it faces a structural market failure. The environmental reservoir (Channel 6) has a system beta of 0.8. This channel-by-channel decomposition is crucial for targeted policy interventions, as it identifies where intervention has the highest welfare return per dollar.

The classification of Slow Hollow Win is robust to significant perturbations of the welfare function. Proposition 1 (Appendix C) demonstrates that if the calibrated aggregate system beta is 2.1 with a welfare shortfall of $930 billion, then the classification holds for any alternative welfare function that differs from the estimated function by less than $465 billion per year. This margin of $465 billion per year is larger than the entire welfare cost of the hospital amplification channel ($280 billion) and the environmental channel ($50 billion) combined. This perturbation robustness means that even if the precise monetization of welfare costs is subject to estimation error, the fundamental conclusion that the antibiotic ecosystem is system-destructive remains unchanged. The robustness is structural, stemming from the concavity of the Private-Systemic Frontier, which ensures that the marginal system cost of private gain accelerates as extraction intensifies.

The Price of Anarchy for the antibiotic prescribing game is 1.35. This metric quantifies the welfare cost incurred due to decentralized, individually rational prescribing behavior compared to a cooperative optimum. The Nash equilibrium of the decentralized prescribing game is maximal prescribing, where each physician prescribes the broadest-spectrum antibiotic to maximize individual patient outcomes. The cooperative optimum involves stewardship-constrained prescribing, such as narrow-spectrum first and escalation only on culture results. The welfare cost at the Nash equilibrium is the full system welfare cost of $3.59 trillion. The welfare cost at the cooperative optimum, representing the irreducible damage from medically necessary use, is $2.66 trillion. The Price of Anarchy of 1.35 indicates that strategic prescribing behavior increases total welfare cost by 35 percent over the cooperative optimum. While this is lower than some other domains, the absolute magnitude of the strategic wedge—$930 billion per year—is enormous and, unlike the constitutive cost, is avoidable through institutional redesign.

Antimicrobial resistance has the largest absolute welfare cost among all domains calibrated in the System Asset Pricing Model program, exceeding all other domains by an order of magnitude. The annual welfare shortfall of $930 billion for antimicrobial resistance is 5.6 times greater than Bitcoin's $165 billion and 4.2 times greater than the combined welfare cost of all four other calibrated domains (Bitcoin, Ethereum, Deep-Sea Mining, and Volkswagen Dieselgate), which total $221 billion. The system-adjusted payoff of -$380 billion is also the largest negative system-adjusted payoff in the program. While the relative system beta of 2.1 is moderate compared to some other domains, the sheer scale of the private payoff base ($550 billion per year) means that even a moderate beta translates into immense absolute welfare destruction. This finding highlights the unparalleled economic significance of the antimicrobial resistance crisis.

Antimicrobial resistance is the only domain among those calibrated in the System Asset Pricing Model program where the commons is absolutely non-renewable. In contrast, Bitcoin's energy problem can be addressed by a shift to renewable mining, Ethereum transitioned from Proof-of-Work to Proof-of-Stake to reduce energy consumption, Volkswagen Dieselgate was resolved by mandating real-world emissions testing, and Deep-Sea Mining's abyssal ecosystems, while recovering on geological timescales, are theoretically recoverable. For antimicrobial resistance, population-level antibiotic effectiveness is governed by evolutionary biology, and resistance genes do not un-evolve. While local resistance can partially revert when selection pressure is removed, as demonstrated by the Danish experience, the global aggregate continues to rise because Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility) ensures that resistance selected anywhere propagates everywhere. This structural non-renewability means that the welfare-destroying trajectory of antimicrobial resistance cannot be reversed by technology or regulation; it can only be slowed.

theorem.formal:
A therapeutic system is defined as a tuple T = (P, D, E, u) consisting of a pathogen population P, an antibiotic drug portfolio D = {d₁, ..., dK}, a population-level effectiveness function E: D × ℝ₊ → [0,1], and a prescribing-rate function u: D × ℝ₊ → ℝ₊, satisfying three conditions: (i) therapeutic action—each drug dk ∈ D kills or inhibits target bacteria through a specific biochemical mechanism (Axiom R1); (ii) evolutionary dynamics—surviving bacteria reproduce resistant offspring on generation timescales of minutes to hours, with reversion bounded below selection at therapeutic doses (Axiom R2); and (iii) resistance mobility—resistance genes transfer horizontally across bacterial species and geographically through travel, trade, food, water, and environmental contamination (Axiom R3). The system class τ denotes the set of all therapeutic systems satisfying (i)–(iii).

Lemma 1 (Selection Accumulation): Under Axiom R1, every therapeutic use u > 0 creates selection pressure s(u) > 0. Under Axiom R2, each selection event irreversibly increases the resistant fraction of the population on clinically relevant timescales. Under Axiom R3, local resistance gains propagate globally. Therefore, cumulative selection pressure S(t) = ∫₀ᵗ s(u(τ))dτ increases monotonically with cumulative use.

Theorem 1 (Efficacy Ceiling): No prescribing protocol, stewardship program, or new antibiotic development pipeline operating within a therapeutic system satisfying Axioms R1 (Therapeutic Lethality), R2 (Evolutionary Selection), and R3 (Resistance Mobility) can guarantee that population-level antibiotic effectiveness remains above any fixed threshold E<sub>min</sub> > 0 as cumulative therapeutic use increases.

Proof: By Lemma 1, cumulative selection pressure S(t) increases monotonically with cumulative use U(t) = ∫₀ᵗ u(τ)dτ. Population-level resistance R(t) is a monotonically increasing function of cumulative selection pressure because Axiom R2 ensures each selection event permanently increases the resistant fraction on clinical timescales. Effectiveness E(t) = 1 − R(t) is therefore monotonically decreasing in cumulative use. For any threshold E<sub>min</sub> > 0, there exists a cumulative use level U* such that E(U*) < E<sub>min</sub>. No prescribing protocol can avoid reaching U* while continuing to provide therapy, because Axiom R1 means any therapeutic use adds to cumulative selection. No stewardship program can reverse R(t), because Axiom R2 means resistance is irreversible on clinical timescales. No new drug pipeline changes E(t) for the depleted class. Axiom R3 ensures local gains are eroded by global resistance propagation. The complete escape-closure apparatus (Propositions EC-2 through EC-8, Section 6.6) systematically proves that no within-system mechanism evades the Efficacy Ceiling. Under the stated dynamics, E(t) = E(0) − ∫₀ᵗ [s(u(τ)) − r(τ)]dτ. Since s(u) > 0 for any u > 0 (Axiom R1), and the reversion rate r is dominated by the selection rate s for every major drug-pathogen combination studied (Axiom R2), the integrand is positive. If cumulative use U(t) = ∫₀ᵗ u(τ)dτ → ∞ (which it must if therapy continues), then ∫₀^∞ s(u(τ))dτ = ∞ while ∫₀^∞ r(τ)dτ is bounded or grows slower. Therefore E(t) → −∞ as t → ∞, and for any threshold E<sub>min</sub> > 0, there exists t* such that E(t*) < E<sub>min</sub>. Introducing class dK+1 creates E<sub>K+1</sub>(0) > 0 but leaves E<sub>k</sub>(t) unchanged for depleted class k. The aggregate E<sub>total</sub>(t) = Σ(k) E<sub>k</sub>(t) can be maintained only if the pipeline rate p (new classes per decade) exceeds the depletion rate d (classes losing clinical viability per decade). Since approximately 1987, p ≈ 0 genuinely novel classes per decade while d ≈ 2–3 classes per decade cross critical resistance thresholds. Since p < d, E<sub>total</sub> declines monotonically. The pipeline creates replacement islands; it does not refill the ocean. ∎

Corollary 1 (Sovereign Necessity): Only sovereign authority operating outside the therapeutic system—with the power to restrict agricultural prophylaxis, mandate global stewardship coordination, and fund pipeline incentives decoupled from market revenue—can slow the depletion rate sufficiently to extend the effectiveness horizon. No voluntary stewardship initiative, hospital protocol, or market-driven pipeline achieves this because each prescriber's optimal strategy under the current commons structure is to prescribe.
theorem.plain:
Imagine a fisherman who catches fish. In a normal fishing commons, the act of catching a fish is separate from the act of depleting the fish population. You could, in theory, catch fish sustainably if the fish reproduce fast enough. But with antibiotics, the situation is fundamentally different. This paper proves the Efficacy Ceiling, an impossibility theorem that says we cannot keep antibiotics working indefinitely at a useful level, no matter what we do within the current system of using them.

This impossibility comes from three basic facts, or axioms, about how antibiotics work and how bacteria behave. Think of them as the unbreakable rules of the game:

First, Axiom R1 (Therapeutic Lethality): Antibiotics work by killing bacteria. This is their job. But here's the catch: the very act of killing bacteria also creates selection pressure. It's like a hunter who, by shooting at a herd, inadvertently trains the survivors to be faster and smarter. The healing and the depletion are the same molecular event. If an antibiotic didn't kill bacteria, it would be a placebo, not an antibiotic.

Second, Axiom R2 (Evolutionary Selection): Bacteria that survive antibiotic exposure reproduce resistant offspring. This is Darwinian natural selection on fast-forward. Bacteria multiply incredibly quickly—sometimes doubling in 20 minutes—and they have huge populations. Even if only a tiny fraction are resistant, those survivors quickly dominate. It's like trying to stop a wildfire by putting out individual sparks; the fire itself is constantly generating new sparks. If bacteria didn't evolve resistance, every antibiotic would work forever, and we would have repealed Darwin.

Third, Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility): Resistance genes don't stay put. They transfer horizontally between different types of bacteria, even across species, through tiny pieces of DNA called plasmids. And they spread geographically through human travel, trade, food, water, and the environment. So, resistance that develops in a pig in China can end up in a patient in Sweden. It's like trying to contain a contagious disease by isolating one person, but the disease can jump across continents and species. If resistance stayed local, we could manage it like a local park, but it's a global ocean.

The Efficacy Ceiling says that because these three axioms are always true, no matter how clever we are with prescribing rules, how many stewardship programs we implement, or how many new drugs we develop, we cannot guarantee that antibiotics will remain effective above a certain minimum threshold as we continue to use them. The system is designed to deplete itself. The best stewardship can only delay the inevitable, not prevent it. New drugs are like building new islands in a shrinking ocean; they don't refill the ocean itself. The impossibility is not a failure of policy; it is a property of biology. The antibiotic cures the patient by killing the bacteria. The bacteria that survive are the ones that will kill the next patient. No within-system intervention separates these two facts.

axioms.name: Therapeutic Lethality (R1)
axioms.statement: For all therapeutic doses u > 0, the selection function s(u) > 0. The selection function is strictly positive for any positive use.
axioms.evidence: Every antibiotic class ever introduced has encountered resistance. Penicillin was introduced clinically in 1943; resistance was reported in 1940 in pre-clinical Staphylococcus isolates. Methicillin was introduced in 1960; methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus was reported in 1961. Vancomycin was introduced in 1958; vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus appeared in 1986. Each subsequent class follows the same pattern, demonstrating the universality of resistance emergence as the empirical content of Axiom R1. If this axiom were dropped, the drug would be a placebo, and the domain would cease to exist.

axioms.name: Evolutionary Selection (R2)
axioms.statement: The resistance dynamics satisfy dR/dt = s(u(t)) − r(R(t)) on clinically relevant timescales, with the reversion rate r bounded by r̄ < ∞, and the selection rate s(u<sub>min</sub>) > r̄ for some minimum therapeutic dose u<sub>min</sub> > 0.
axioms.evidence: The speed of resistance emergence itself provides evidence of Axiom R2's force. Bacterial generation times range from 20 minutes for Escherichia coli to 24 hours for Mycobacterium tuberculosis. With population sizes of 10⁹ to 10¹² in a single infected host and mutation rates of approximately 10⁻⁹ per base pair per generation, resistance mutations arise spontaneously at predictable frequencies. Selection amplifies them on timescales of days to weeks within a single patient and months to years within a population. Resistance genes often carry minimal fitness costs and can persist for decades after selection pressure is removed, making the biological mechanism irreversible on clinically relevant timescales. If this axiom were dropped, every antibiotic would work forever, effectively repealing Darwinian evolution.

axioms.name: Resistance Mobility (R3)
axioms.statement: Effectiveness E(t) is a global variable: dE<sub>j</sub>/dt depends on Σ(k) g<sub>jk</sub> R<sub>k</sub>(t) for all jurisdictions j, with mobility coefficients g<sub>jk</sub> > 0 for all j ≠ k.
axioms.evidence: The global spread of mobile colistin resistance gene MCR-1, discovered in Chinese livestock in 2015 and detected on five continents within 18 months, is a paradigm case for Axiom R3. Class 1 integrons carrying multiple resistance cassettes are found in virtually every environment surveyed. Plasmids can transfer resistance between bacterial species that diverged hundreds of millions of years ago. The environmental resistome, the total pool of resistance genes in environmental bacteria, contains diverse resistance determinants that predate modern antibiotic use but become clinically relevant when mobilized via horizontal gene transfer into human pathogens. If this axiom were dropped, resistance would stay local, and the commons would become manageable at a local level, effectively repealing microbial genetics.

theorem.proofSketch:
The proof of the Efficacy Ceiling (Theorem 1) proceeds through a series of logical steps, building upon the three defining axioms of antibiotic therapy: Therapeutic Lethality (R1), Evolutionary Selection (R2), and Resistance Mobility (R3). The core argument establishes that cumulative antibiotic use inevitably leads to a decline in population-level effectiveness below any fixed threshold, and that no within-system intervention can prevent this. The proof follows the standard architecture for impossibility theorems: state independently necessary axioms, prove their conjunction implies impossibility, and show dropping any one axiom permits the objective.

Step 1: Selection Accumulation (Lemma 1). This lemma establishes that cumulative selection pressure increases monotonically with cumulative antibiotic use. Axiom R1 (Therapeutic Lethality) states that any therapeutic dose of an antibiotic, by its very nature of killing or inhibiting bacteria, creates selection pressure for resistant organisms. This means that for any positive use (u > 0), the selection function s(u) is strictly positive. As long as antibiotic therapy continues indefinitely, the integral of this positive selection pressure over time, ∫₀ᵗ s(u(τ))dτ, will necessarily accumulate to infinity. This step is crucial because it links the act of using antibiotics directly to the continuous generation of resistance. Without R1, an antibiotic would be a placebo, and the domain would cease to exist.

Step 2: Bounded Reversion. Axiom R2 (Evolutionary Selection) acknowledges that while bacteria evolve resistance, there might be some natural reversion where resistant strains with fitness costs are outcompeted by susceptible ones if selection pressure is removed. However, R2 also states that this reversion rate (r) is bounded above by a finite value (r̄). This means that reversion cannot indefinitely offset the continuous selection pressure created by ongoing antibiotic use. The rate at which resistance is generated by therapeutic use (s(u)) is empirically greater than the maximum rate at which resistance might revert (r̄) for any minimum therapeutic dose (u<sub>min</sub>). Therefore, the net effect of antibiotic use is always an increase in resistance. If R2 were false, and reversion could always outpace selection, then antibiotics would work forever, effectively repealing Darwinian evolution.

Step 3: Net Accumulation of Resistance. Combining Step 1 and Step 2, the change in population-level resistance R(t) over time is given by dR/dt = s(u(t)) − r(R(t)). Since Axiom R2 ensures that s(u<sub>min</sub>) > r̄, the net rate of resistance accumulation (s(u(t)) − r(R(t))) is strictly positive whenever antibiotics are used at therapeutic levels. Over an infinite horizon of continued therapy, the positive contributions from selection will always dominate the bounded contributions from reversion. This implies that population-level resistance R(t) will monotonically increase over time, eventually approaching 1 (complete resistance).

Step 4: Threshold Violation. Since effectiveness E(t) is defined as 1 − R(t), and R(t) is monotonically increasing and approaches 1, it follows that E(t) is monotonically decreasing and approaches 0. Therefore, for any fixed threshold of minimum effectiveness (E<sub>min</sub> > 0) that is clinically relevant, there will inevitably come a time (T*) when the actual effectiveness E(T*) falls below E<sub>min</sub>. This means the drug or class of drugs will become therapeutically unusable. This step directly proves the 'ceiling' aspect of the theorem: effectiveness cannot be maintained above any positive threshold indefinitely.

Step 5: No Within-System Escape. This is where the escape-closure apparatus (Propositions EC-2 through EC-15) comes into play, systematically demonstrating that no intervention operating *within* the defined therapeutic system can circumvent the Efficacy Ceiling. The core reason for these failures is Axiom R1: the therapeutic effect and the depletion effect are the same molecular event, making it impossible to separate cure from selection pressure. Let's detail some of these failures:

*   **Stewardship Impossibility (EC-2):** Even an optimally designed and perfectly enforced stewardship protocol (M*) cannot guarantee E(t) ≥ E<sub>min</sub>. While stewardship minimizes unnecessary use, it still requires a minimum therapeutic dose (u<sub>min</sub>) to treat patients. By R1, this u<sub>min</sub> still creates selection pressure (s(u<sub>min</sub>) > 0), leading to continuous, albeit slower, accumulation of resistance. The best stewardship delays the Ceiling; it does not lift it. The delay is measured in decades. The impossibility is measured in molecular biology.
*   **Portfolio Impossibility (EC-3) and No Pipeline Escape (EC-3 Corollary):** Strategies like cycling, mixing, or rotating different antibiotic classes also fail. Each class, independently, is subject to the same R1-R2 depletion dynamic. Furthermore, R3 (Resistance Mobility) ensures that resistance genes can transfer between bacterial species and often carry resistance to multiple drug classes (cross-resistance or co-selection). So, using one drug can select for resistance to another. A new drug pipeline, while introducing new classes, does not restore effectiveness to depleted classes. If the rate of new drug discovery (p) is less than the rate of existing drug depletion (d), which has been the case since approximately 1987 (p ≈ 0, d ≈ 2-3 classes per decade), then aggregate effectiveness will still decline. The pipeline creates replacement islands; it does not refill the ocean. The clinician rotates the drugs. The bacteria rotate the plasmids. R3 ensures the bacteria rotate faster.
*   **Partial Coalition Insufficiency (EC-4):** Even if a subset of jurisdictions (S) implements optimal stewardship, the non-coalition jurisdictions (N\S) continue to generate resistance. Axiom R3 ensures that this resistance propagates globally, reaching the coalition members through travel, trade, and environmental pathways. Therefore, global effectiveness cannot be preserved unless all jurisdictions participate (S = N). Denmark proved that local stewardship works locally. EC-4 proves that local stewardship cannot work globally. The distance between the two statements is Axiom R3.
*   **No Prescribing Mechanism Without Sovereign Surveillance (EC-5):** Any prescribing mechanism based solely on patient-level clinical information (symptoms, local resistance data) cannot protect global effectiveness. This is because population-level effectiveness E(t) is not contained in individual patient records. Without an independent, population-level surveillance channel—real-time resistance data aggregated across jurisdictions—such a mechanism optimizes patient outcomes while being structurally blind to commons depletion. The prescriber sees the patient. The commons sees the prescriber. These are different information sets. No mechanism operating within the first can protect the second without a bridge between them. That bridge is population-level surveillance.
*   **Repeated Stewardship Cooperation Does Not Escape (EC-6):** Even if jurisdictions could sustain cooperative equilibria in an infinitely repeated game through game-theoretic mechanisms like Folk Theorem trigger strategies, the fundamental biological constraint remains. As long as therapy continues, positive prescribing generates positive selection pressure, and cumulative selection diverges, leading to E(t) → 0. The Folk Theorem gave the jurisdictions a coordination device. It did not give them an escape from the Ceiling. The repetition extends the time horizon of depletion. It does not change its structural character.
*   **Depletion Rate Bound and the Welfare Gap (EC-7):** This proposition quantifies the rate of depletion, showing that E(t) ≤ E(0) − ε · U(t) + r̄ · t, where ε is the net selection rate and U(t) is cumulative use. This directly implies that the system beta for appropriate prescribing (βW) is necessarily greater than zero, meaning every therapeutically effective prescription depletes the commons. The system-adjusted payoff is negative not because of empirical calibration choices but because the axioms guarantee it. The impossibility theorem does not merely predict that the commons will deplete. It computes the minimum rate. That rate is βW dollars of welfare per dollar of private gain, and it is bounded below by the molecular biology.

In summary, the proof demonstrates that the Efficacy Ceiling is a structural impossibility inherent in the biological nature of antibiotic action and bacterial evolution. The interventions that might seem intuitive (stewardship, new drugs) can only slow the process, not fundamentally alter its trajectory within the confines of Axioms R1, R2, and R3. The only interventions that truly escape the Ceiling operate *outside* the therapeutic system class τ, such as vaccines or infection control, which do not kill bacteria and therefore do not create selection pressure. Six escape routes. Six structural failures. One molecular event. The antibiotic cures the patient by killing the bacteria. The bacteria that survive are the ones that will kill the next patient. No within-system intervention separates these two facts.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $550 billion per year, representing the aggregate private payoff from human therapeutic antibiotic market revenue, veterinary antibiotic market revenue, the immediate economic value of infections cured, and the economic value of surgeries, transplants, chemotherapy, and neonatal care enabled by antibiotic prophylaxis.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $930 billion per year, representing the welfare shortfall—the gap between the cooperative welfare maximum and the realized system welfare, priced at a rate of one dollar per dollar of welfare.

channels.name: Inappropriate Clinical Prescribing
channels.description: This channel captures the welfare cost associated with antibiotic prescriptions that are medically unnecessary, such as those for viral infections, self-limiting conditions, or without diagnostic justification. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimates that approximately 30 percent of outpatient antibiotic prescriptions in the United States are unnecessary, with global figures potentially higher. Each unnecessary prescription creates selection pressure for resistant organisms with zero genuine therapeutic benefit, representing pure commons depletion. The welfare cost is estimated by capitalizing the per-prescription external cost of resistance over a long horizon, reflecting the permanent increase in the resistant fraction of the population under Axiom R2. Against a perceived private benefit of approximately $65 billion globally, this channel exhibits the highest system beta of 5.2, meaning each dollar of imagined benefit destroys $5.20 of the effectiveness commons. The denominator is a fiction. The numerator is permanent.

channels.name: Appropriate Clinical Prescribing
channels.description: Even medically necessary prescriptions deplete the effectiveness commons, as established by Axiom R1 (Therapeutic Lethality). This channel quantifies the welfare cost generated by clinically justified antibiotic use. While the per-prescription welfare cost is lower than for inappropriate prescribing due to the genuine therapeutic benefit, the aggregate volume of appropriate use is enormous, estimated at approximately 14.5 billion defined daily doses per year globally. This makes it the largest contributor to the absolute system welfare cost. The system beta for this channel is 0.57, which is below unit Private-Systemic Tension, but its sheer scale means it drives a significant portion of the overall depletion. The cost is measured using per-standard-unit resistance cost estimates from the cross-country costing literature, reflecting the inevitable selection pressure created by every effective dose. This is the channel where the Efficacy Ceiling bites hardest: you cannot eliminate the depletion from appropriate use without eliminating the therapy.

channels.name: Livestock Prophylaxis
channels.description: This channel accounts for the welfare cost of antibiotic use in healthy animals for growth promotion and disease prevention, which often exceeds human therapeutic use by volume. The World Organisation for Animal Health reports approximately 74,000 tonnes of antimicrobials used in animals globally in 2022, with a significant portion being medically important antibiotics. The private payoff from this use, primarily marginal productivity gains, is estimated at $10 billion per year. The system welfare cost is derived from the fraction of human antimicrobial resistance burden causally attributable to livestock prophylaxis, estimated at a central attribution fraction of 2 percent. This channel has a high system beta of 2.5, indicating that each dollar of private payoff generates $2.50 of system welfare cost. This high beta is due to the marginal private benefit, the clinical consequences of resistance transfer to humans (e.g., MCR-1 colistin resistance), and the use of last-resort drugs in agriculture. Feed an animal a last-resort antibiotic so it gains weight faster. Lose the drug that saves human lives. That is a $2.50 exchange rate and Denmark proved it was optional.

channels.name: Pipeline Depletion
channels.description: This channel quantifies the welfare cost associated with the failure to replace antibiotic classes that lose effectiveness to resistance. The antibiotic pipeline is structurally broken: development costs are high ($1.2 billion to $2.6 billion per drug), but annual sales for patented antibiotics are low, leading to commercial failures like Achaogen and Melinta Therapeutics. The pipeline produces approximately 17 new systemic antibiotics per decade, mostly modifications of existing classes, while resistance consumes old ones faster. The welfare cost includes both direct development expenditure and the welfare value of therapeutic options that were never created. Against the pharmaceutical industry's revenue from antibiotic sales, the pipeline channel's system beta is 0.55, which is below unit Private-Systemic Tension in isolation. However, this understates the structural problem: the broken market means that even developing new classes is a Hollow Win, as new drugs are prescribed into resistance under the same commons structure that depleted their predecessors.

channels.name: Hospital Resistance Amplification
channels.description: Hospitals are hotspots where resistant organisms concentrate, evolve, and spread. Intensive care unit patients receive heavy antibiotic exposure, creating intense selection pressure. Hospital-acquired resistant infections, such as methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales, and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus, lead to higher mortality, longer hospital stays, and increased costs. The global hospital costs attributable to resistance are estimated at $693 billion per year. This channel's system beta is 2.3, reflecting the amplification effect: hospitals do not merely reflect population-level resistance but accelerate its evolution and spread. The hospital environment, with its fluctuating antibiotic concentrations and patient-to-patient transmission, acts as an incubator. The hospital is simultaneously the beneficiary of effective antibiotics and the incubator of resistance that destroys their effectiveness—a structural self-cannibalization that mirrors the broader commons dynamic at the institutional scale.

channels.name: Environmental Reservoir
channels.description: This channel accounts for the welfare cost arising from environmental reservoirs of resistance genes. Pharmaceutical manufacturing effluent, hospital wastewater, and agricultural runoff create environments where resistance genes accumulate, recombine, and transfer between environmental and human-pathogenic bacteria. The Hyderabad manufacturing region provides an extreme example, with ciprofloxacin concentrations in effluent more than 500,000 times the resistance-selection threshold. Approximately 8,500 tonnes of human-consumed antibiotics enter river systems annually, with millions of kilometers of rivers exceeding resistance-promotion thresholds. Against an estimated economic value of $30 billion from the activities generating this contamination, the environmental channel has an average system beta of 0.8. While the irreversibility of resistance genes in environmental reservoirs is absolute, the per-dollar rate is moderate due to the substantial private payoff base and the uncertainty in quantifying the environmental contribution to human infection. This is recognized as the least precisely quantified channel.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The robustness of the System Asset Pricing Model classification for antimicrobial resistance is rigorously tested through a Monte Carlo simulation involving 100,000 parameter draws. This extensive simulation confirms that the classification of Slow Hollow Win with catastrophic discontinuity risk is invariant to reasonable variations in the underlying parameters. The simulation uses a seed of 42 for reproducibility. The methodology involves drawing parameter vectors from defined ranges for each of the six welfare channels. Channel betas are drawn from triangular distributions, anchored to the low, central, and high estimates presented in Table 3. This distribution type is chosen to reflect expert judgment on the most likely values while allowing for a plausible range of uncertainty. The payoff weights for each channel are held constant at their adopted channel shares, as these are derived from the aggregate private payoff decomposition. Initially, independent draws are used for each channel. This produces a weighted average system beta with a mean of 1.60 and a 90 percent confidence interval of [1.27, 1.95]. Mapping these through the concave Private-Systemic Frontier yields a marginal system beta distribution centered near 2.1, with a 90 percent confidence interval of [1.6, 2.6]. A critical finding from this initial run is that zero of the 100,000 draws produce a welfare-positive aggregate outcome. This means that under no plausible combination of parameter values, within the defined ranges, does the antibiotic ecosystem generate more system welfare than it depletes. Recognizing that the six channels of Private-Systemic Tension are not causally independent and share common pathogen populations and resistance gene pools, a second, more robust simulation is performed using a correlated-draw structure. This correlation structure is derived from the causal directed acyclic graph presented in Section 3.4. Specific correlations are imposed: * A correlation coefficient of 0.5 between the livestock prophylaxis channel (Channel 3) and the environmental reservoir channel (Channel 6), reflecting how agricultural antimicrobial runoff contaminates waterways. * A correlation coefficient of 0.4 between the inappropriate clinical prescribing channel (Channel 1) and the hospital resistance amplification channel (Channel 5), acknowledging that unnecessary outpatient resistance enters hospitals via colonized patients. * A correlation coefficient of 0.6 between the hospital resistance amplification channel (Channel 5) and the mortality welfare costs (Wmortality), as hospital-acquired resistant infections are the primary driver of antimicrobial resistance-attributable deaths. * A correlation coefficient of 0.2 is applied to all other channel pairs, reflecting a general shared pathogen ecology and interconnectedness. These correlated draws are generated using a Cholesky decomposition of the 6x6 correlation matrix, applied to the marginal triangular distributions of the channel betas. The results from the correlated Monte Carlo simulation show a marginal system beta distribution with an unchanged mean of 2.1. However, the 90 percent confidence interval widens to [1.4, 2.9], compared to [1.6, 2.6] under the independence assumption. Similarly, the weighted-average beta has a 90 percent interval of [1.15, 2.10], compared to [1.27, 1.95] under independence. Critically, the classification remains invariant: zero of the 100,000 correlated draws produce a welfare-positive aggregate. The 5th percentile of the marginal system beta distribution, even at 1.4, still implies a system-adjusted payoff that is negative at any shadow price above $0.85. This demonstrates that while accounting for inter-channel correlation affects the uncertainty bounds, it does not alter the fundamental sign of the welfare verdict. Sensitivity analysis further confirms the robustness of the classification. The Slow Hollow Win classification holds for any welfare function W' with a maximum difference from the estimated welfare function W (sup||W' − W||) less than $465 billion per year (Proposition 1, Appendix C). This margin is larger than the entire hospital amplification and environmental channels combined, indicating that the classification is not sensitive to precise mortality valuation or other welfare function parameters. Even at the most conservative mortality valuation ($0.5 million per death), the aggregate welfare shortfall substantially exceeds the private payoff, and the marginal system beta falls to approximately 1.4, still above unity. The uncertainty is about the magnitude of the welfare gap, not about its sign or its classification. The Monte Carlo results provide compelling evidence that the system beta is above 1.0 in every plausible scenario, making the Slow Hollow Win classification a topological feature of the Private-Systemic Frontier, not merely a point estimate.

caseStudies.title: The Nevada 2016 Pan-Resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae Case
caseStudies.content: In August 2016, a woman in Nevada was admitted to a hospital with a severe infection caused by Klebsiella pneumoniae. This particular strain of bacteria was found to be resistant to all 26 antibiotics available in the United States, including last-resort drugs like colistin and carbapenems. The patient, who had recently returned from India where she had been hospitalized for a prolonged period due to a leg fracture, had acquired the infection there. Despite aggressive medical intervention, she died in September 2016. This case is a stark illustration of the 'catastrophic discontinuity risk' inherent in the Slow Hollow Win classification of the antibiotic ecosystem. While aggregate antibiotic effectiveness declines gradually across many drug-pathogen combinations, individual drug-pathogen pairs can cross clinical thresholds discontinuously, leading to abrupt and untreatable infections. The Nevada case was not a future scenario; it was a present fact, demonstrating that the 'step function' of antibiotic failure had already stepped for one patient in one hospital in one country. This event directly validates the stochastic Efficacy Ceiling (Proposition EC-14), which formalizes how crossover times for individual drug-pathogen combinations can have heavy left tails, meaning that for some patients, the 'crossover' to untreatability arrives much sooner than the aggregate average. The patient's infection was caused by a New Delhi metallo-beta-lactamase-producing carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales, a pathogen classified as 'Critical' priority by the World Health Organization. The global spread of such highly resistant organisms, often originating in regions with high antibiotic use and limited sanitation, exemplifies Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility), where resistance genes transfer geographically through travel and trade. The system that prescribed those drugs is still prescribing. The commons that failed her is still being spent.

caseStudies.title: The Danish Exception: A Documented Game Transformation in Livestock Antibiotic Use
caseStudies.content: Denmark's experience with agricultural antibiotic regulation stands as the clearest documented example of a game transformation from a Hollow Win towards a more sustainable equilibrium in a specific channel. In 1995, Denmark banned the use of avoparcin, a vancomycin analog used as a growth promoter in poultry and swine. This was followed by a ban on virginiamycin in 1998, and by 2000, all remaining antibiotic growth promoters were voluntarily withdrawn by Danish producers, preceding a formal European Union-wide ban in 2006. This series of interventions led to a greater than 50 percent reduction in total livestock antibiotic use in Denmark between 1994 and 2008. Crucially, this reduction was achieved with minimal impact on agricultural productivity. Minor short-term costs, primarily in weaner pig production, were fully offset within two to three years through improved management practices, better biosecurity, and targeted therapeutic use when clinically warranted. The Danish Integrated Antimicrobial Resistance Monitoring Programme (DANMAP) provided robust longitudinal surveillance data, documenting measurable declines in vancomycin-resistant enterococci prevalence in broilers and pigs after avoparcin withdrawal. By 2020, Danish pig production reported practically no use of fluoroquinolones, third- or fourth-generation cephalosporins, or polymyxins—antibiotics classified as critically important by the World Health Organization. In System Asset Pricing Model terms, Denmark did not repeal the biological axioms of antibiotic action. Instead, it changed the game by removing a large block of low-value extraction from the livestock channel, thereby flattening the Private-Systemic Frontier locally. The intervention targeted the channel with the highest system beta (livestock prophylaxis, 2.5), demonstrating that reducing extraction where the marginal system cost per dollar of private gain is steepest yields the highest welfare return. This is exactly what the shadow price duality (Proposition 16) would recommend. While Denmark's success is local, Proposition EC-4 (Partial Coalition Insufficiency) highlights that global coordination is necessary to fully escape the Efficacy Ceiling due to Resistance Mobility (R3). Nevertheless, the Danish model provides compelling empirical evidence that game transformation works at a national scale, proving that the geometry of the frontier can be bent by policy.

caseStudies.title: MCR-1 and Colistin: The Rapid Depletion of a Last-Resort Antibiotic
caseStudies.content: The emergence and global spread of mobile colistin resistance gene MCR-1 exemplifies the rapid depletion dynamics of a last-resort antibiotic, directly illustrating Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility) and the catastrophic discontinuity risk of the Efficacy Ceiling. Colistin (polymyxin E) had been used extensively in livestock across Asia, South America, and parts of Europe as a growth promoter and for prophylaxis. In human medicine, it was a terminal therapeutic option for multi-drug-resistant Gram-negative infections, used sparingly due to toxicity. In November 2015, researchers in China characterized MCR-1, a plasmid-mediated gene conferring colistin resistance, found in Escherichia coli isolates from Chinese food animals, retail meat, and hospitalized patients. This discovery was seismic because it demonstrated that colistin resistance, previously thought to arise only from chromosomal mutations, could transfer horizontally between bacterial species via plasmids. Within 18 months, MCR-1 was detected on five continents, in clinical, animal, food, and environmental samples. This rapid, global dissemination of a resistance gene to a last-resort antibiotic, driven by agricultural use, highlights the severe Private-Systemic Tension in the livestock prophylaxis channel (system beta of 2.5). The private payoff from colistin use in agriculture (marginal growth promotion) was small, but the system welfare cost of losing this terminal drug was immense: the potential for untreatable pan-resistant infections. China responded by banning colistin sulfate premix as a growth promoter in April 2017, followed by India in July 2019. Despite these bans, MCR-positive isolates continue to be detected globally, demonstrating that once resistance genes are mobilized via plasmids, they persist and spread even after selection pressure is removed, often through co-selection with other resistance genes. The colistin story is a miniature of the Efficacy Ceiling: selection pressure applied for a marginal private gain created an irreversible welfare cost that propagated globally in less than two years. The healing and the depletion are the same molecular event. For colistin, the depletion was agricultural, and the healing it destroyed was clinical.

policyAnalysis.currentFramework:
The current regulatory and legal framework for addressing antimicrobial resistance is extensive on paper but fragmented and incomplete in implementation. The World Health Organization Global Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance, adopted in 2015, has been endorsed by 178 countries, leading to the development of national action plans. However, only 27 percent of these national action plans are fully costed and funded, indicating a significant gap between commitment and execution. The 2024 United Nations High-Level Meeting on Antimicrobial Resistance produced a Political Declaration with specific targets, including a 10 percent reduction in antimicrobial resistance deaths by 2030 and 60 percent of countries having funded national action plans. However, this declaration stopped short of creating binding legal obligations, leaving enforcement largely to individual sovereign states. Jurisdiction over antimicrobial resistance spans multiple agencies and sectors. In human health, national health ministries and public health agencies (e.g., the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention in the United States, the European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control in the European Union) are responsible for surveillance, stewardship guidelines, and infection prevention. In the agricultural sector, ministries of agriculture and veterinary authorities (e.g., the World Organisation for Animal Health) regulate antibiotic use in livestock. Environmental protection agencies are increasingly recognized as having a role in monitoring antibiotic residues and resistance genes in water and soil. Pharmaceutical regulation falls under agencies like the Food and Drug Administration in the United States and the European Medicines Agency in the European Union. Key laws and regulations include the European Union's Regulation 2019/6 on veterinary medicinal products, which became applicable in 2022 and includes stricter rules on antimicrobial use in animals, including a ban on growth promoters. The United Kingdom's National Health Service has launched an antibiotic subscription model, awarding contracts to pharmaceutical manufacturers to decouple payment from prescribing volume, aiming to incentivize novel antibiotic development. Sweden has implemented a similar revenue guarantee pilot. In the United States, the Food and Drug Administration's Guidance #213, implemented by 2017, removed production uses for medically important antibiotics in livestock. Initiatives like Combating Antibiotic Resistant Bacteria Biopharmaceutical Accelerator and the Global Antibiotic Research & Development Partnership represent institutional innovations aimed at addressing the broken antibiotic pipeline through public-private partnerships. Despite these frameworks, the lack of a binding international treaty means that global coordination remains voluntary and often insufficient to address the transboundary nature of resistance, as formalized by Axiom R3 (Resistance Mobility). The existing regulatory landscape illustrates both the possibility and the limits of Tier 3 action, where sovereign authority exists in principle but enforcement is fragmented and incomplete. The biological clock of resistance evolution, operating on timescales of minutes to hours, runs significantly faster than the institutional response, which often operates on timescales of years to decades.
